critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT IGNORES TEMPORAL DISTRIBUTION SHIFT IN CLINICAL CODING. Radiology reports collected over two years will reflect changes in ICD coding practices, hospital policy updates, departmental staffing, and scanner technology. A stratified random split treats the 10,000 reports as exchangeable draws from a stationary distribution. If coding practices shifted (e.g., ICD-10 revision, new disease code adoption), reports in the test set may carry label distributions not present in training in proportion. The design acknowledges cross-departmental variation as a confound but not temporal label drift. Evidence: compare model performance when trained on the earliest 70% of reports and evaluated on the most recent 15% versus the random split performance; a large performance gap reveals temporal non-stationarity.

2. MACRO-F1 IS SENSITIVE TO RARE LABEL SET COMPOSITION AND IS NOT VALIDATED AGAINST CLINICAL PRIORITY. The design uses Macro-F1 to 'evenly assess performance across all labels.' In a multi-label clinical coding setting with hundreds of possible ICD codes, many codes will appear only a handful of times. Macro-F1 treats a single-instance disease code identically to a high-frequency code. The model could achieve acceptable Macro-F1 by performing well on the moderate-frequency codes while failing entirely on the clinically most consequential rare codes. No evidence is provided that the label set composition is validated against clinical priority or that rare labels have reliable enough annotations to make their contribution to Macro-F1 meaningful. Evidence: report per-label F1 distributions and show that high-Macro-F1 corresponds to acceptable performance on clinically prioritized codes.

3. RULE-BASED BASELINE IS NOT A STRONG ENOUGH COMPARATOR FOR THE MULTI-LABEL SETTING. A keyword extraction rule-based system is described as the baseline with 'equivalent effort for fairness.' However, for multi-label classification with hundreds of codes, a well-engineered rule-based system with synonym expansion, negation handling, and uncertainty detection (as used in production clinical NLP systems) is far more capable than a plain keyword match. The design does not specify the sophistication of the rule-based system. If the baseline is a naive keyword list, the comparison does not establish superiority over the relevant practitioner alternative. Evidence: benchmark against a published clinical NLP system (e.g., NegEx + keyword) and show the transformer margin holds.

4. FINE-TUNING SCOPE AND CATASTROPHIC FORGETTING RISK ARE UNADDRESSED. The design says the transformer is 'fine-tuned on annotated radiology reports' but does not specify which layers are fine-tuned, the learning rate schedule, or whether the pre-trained model was already adapted to clinical text (e.g., BioBERT, ClinicalBERT) or trained on general text. A general-domain BERT fine-tuned on 10,000 radiology reports risks either catastrophic forgetting (if full fine-tuning is used) or underfitting (if only the head is trained). The choice here materially affects the validity of the performance claim. Evidence: ablate full fine-tuning vs. head-only vs. domain-adapted base model to isolate the source of any performance gain.

5. STRATIFICATION BY DEPARTMENT AS A CONFOUND CONTROL IS INSUFFICIENT WITHOUT HOLDOUT ANALYSIS. The design controls for 'variations due to departmental coding practices' by stratifying splits by department. However, stratified sampling ensures representation but does not control for department-level systematic differences. If Department A uses one coding convention and Department B uses another, a model trained on both will blend these signals. The correct test of generalization is a leave-one-department-out evaluation. The current design cannot distinguish a model that generalizes across departments from one that memorizes each department's idiosyncratic coding style. Evidence: run leave-one-department-out cross-validation and compare to the stratified random split performance.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores temporal label drift from ICD coding practice changes
Macro-F1 sensitivity to rare label composition without clinical priority validation
Rule-based baseline is insufficiently specified and may be an unrepresentative weak comparator
Fine-tuning scope and base model domain adaptation are unspecified, affecting interpretation of performance gains
Stratified department sampling does not constitute cross-department generalization control
Stratified random split ignores radiologist-specific and temporal coding drift
Macro-F1 instability for multi-label tasks with large, heavy-tailed label spaces
Rule-based baseline has categorical structural advantage on frequent, explicitly named diseases that may not be visible in aggregate metrics
Per-label training sample size relative to label cardinality is not established as a precondition
Departmental stratification controls for confound post-hoc but does not test generalization to unseen departments
Random split on clinical records ignores temporal shifts in radiologist language and coding practice
Annotation quality and inter-annotator reliability are uncharacterized, making labels unreliable as ground truth
Rule-based baseline construction effort is undefined, risking a straw-man comparison
Macro-F1 is unstable under rare disease code imbalance in a multi-label setting
Department-stratified subgroup analysis is underpowered given dataset size
